chapters.title: The Prologue
chapters.content:
The Prologue
			
				When that Aprilis, with his showers swoot,1
				

				The drought of March hath pierced to the root,
				

				And bathed every vein in such licĂłur,
				

				Of which virtĂşe engenderâd is the flower;
				

				When Zephyrus eke with his swootĂŤ breath
				

				Inspired hath in every holt2 and heath
				

				The tender croppĂŤs,3 and the youngĂŤ sun
				

				Hath in the Ram4 his halfĂŤ course y-run,
				

				And smallĂŤ fowlĂŤs makĂŤ melody,
				

				That sleepen all the night with open eye,
				

				(So pricketh them natĂşre in their corĂĄges5);
				

				Then longĂŤ folk to go on pilgrimages,
				

				And palmers6 for to seekĂŤ strangĂŤ strands,
				

				To fernĂŤ hallows couth7 in sundry lands;
				

				And specially, from every shirĂŤâs end
				

				Of Engleland, to Canterbury they wend,
				

				The holy blissful Martyr for to seek,
				

				That them hath holpen, when that they were sick.
			

			
				Befell that, in that season on a day,
				

				In Southwark at the Tabard8 as I lay,
				

				Ready to wenden on my pilgrimage
				

				To Canterbury with devout corĂĄge,
				

				At night was come into that hostelry
				

				Well nine and twenty in a company
				

				Of sundry folk, by ĂĄventure y-fall
				

				In fellowship,9 and pilgrims were they all,
				

				That toward Canterbury wouldĂŤ ride.
				

				The chamber, and the stables werĂŤ wide,
				

				And well we weren eased at the best.10
				

				And shortly, when the sunnĂŤ was to rest,
				

				So had I spoken with them every one,
				

				That I was of their fellowship anon,
				

				And madĂŤ forword11 early for to rise,
				

				To take our way there as I you devise.12
			

			
				But natheless, while I have time and space,
				

				Ere that I farther in this talĂŤ pace,
				

				Me thinketh it accordant to reasĂłn,
				

				To tell you allĂŤ the condition
				

				Of each of them, so as it seemed me,
				

				And which they weren, and of what degree;
				

				And eke in what array that they were in:
				

				And at a Knight then will I first begin.
			

			
				A Knight there was, and that a worthy man,
				

				That from the timĂŤ that he first began
				

				To riden out, he loved chivalry,
				

				Truth and honoĂşr, freedom and courtesy.
				

				Full worthy was he in his LordĂŤâs war,
				

				And thereto had he ridden, no man farre,13
				

				As well in Christendom as in Heatheness,
				

				And ever honourâd for his worthiness.
				

				At Alisandre14 he was when it was won.
				

				Full often time he had the board begun
				

				Above allĂŤ natiĂłns in Prusse.15
				

				In Lettowe had he reysed,16 and in Russe,
				

				No Christian man so oft of his degree.
				

				In Grenade at the siege eke had he be
				

				Of Algesir,17 and ridden in Belmarie.18
				

				At LeyĂŤs was he, and at Satalie,
				

				When they were won; and in the GreatĂŤ Sea19
				

				At many a noble army had he be.
				

				At mortal battles had he been fifteen,
				

				And foughten for our faith at Tramissene.20
				

				In listĂŤs thriĂŤs, and aye slain his foe.
				

				This ilkĂŤ21 worthy knight had been also
				

				Some timĂŤ with the lord of Palatie,22
				

				Against another heathen in Turkie:
				

				And evermore he had a sovereign price.23
				

				And though that he was worthy he was wise,
				

				And of his port as meek as is a maid.
				

				He never yet no villainy24 ne said
				

				In all his life, unto no manner wight.
				

				He was a very perfect gentle knight.
				

				But for to tellĂŤ you of his array,
				

				His horse was good, but yet he was not gay.
				

				Of fustian he weared a gipon,
				

				AllĂŤ besmotterâd with his habergeon,25
				

				For he was late y-come from his voyĂĄge,
				

				And wentĂŤ for to do his pilgrimage.
			

			
			
				With him there was his son, a youngĂŤ Squire,
				

				A lover, and a lusty bacheler,
				

				With lockĂŤs crulle26 as they were laid in press.
				

				Of twenty year of age he was I guess.
				

				Of his statĂşre he was of even length,
				

				And wonderly deliver,27 and great of strength.
				

				And he had been some time in chevachie,28
				

				In Flanders, in Artois, and Picardie,
				

				And borne him well, as of so little space,29
				

				In hope to standen in his ladyâs grace.
				

				Embroiderâd was he, as it were a mead
				

				All full of freshĂŤ flowers, white and red.
				

				Singing he was, or fluting all the day;
				

				He was as fresh as is the month of May.
				

				Short was his gown, with sleevĂŤs long and wide.
				

				Well could he sit on horse, and fairĂŤ ride.
				

				He couldĂŤ songĂŤs make, and well indite,
				

				Joust, and eke dance, and well pourtray and write.
				

				So hot he loved, that by nightertale30
				

				He slept no more than doth the nightingale.
				

				Courteous he was, lowly, and serviceable,
				

				And carvâd before his father at the table.31
			

			
			
				A Yeoman had he, and servĂĄnts no moâ
				

				At that timĂŤ, for him list ridĂŤ so;32
				

				And he was clad in coat and hood of green.
				

				A sheaf of peacock arrows33 bright and keen
				

				Under his belt he bare full thriftily.
				

				Well could he dress his tackle yeomanly:
				

				His arrows drooped not with feathers low;
				

				And in his hand he bare a mighty bow.
				

				A nut-head34 had he, with a brown visĂĄge:
				

				Of wood-craft coud35 he well all the usĂĄge:
				

				Upon his arm he bare a gay bracĂŠr,36
				

				And by his side a sword and a bucklĂŠr,
				

				And on that other side a gay daggere,
				

				Harnessed well, and sharp as point of spear:
				

				A Christopher37 on his breast of silver sheen.
				

				An horn he bare, the baldric was of green:
				

				A forester38 was he soothly39 as I guess.
			

			
			
				There was also a Nun, a Prioress,
				

				That of her smiling was full simple and coy;
				

				Her greatest oathĂŤ was but by Saint Loy;40
				

				And she was cleped41 Madame Eglentine.
				

				Full well she sang the servicĂŤ divine,
				

				Entuned in her nose full seemĂŤly;42
				

				And French she spake full fair and fetisly43
				

				After the school of Stratford attĂŤ Bow,
				

				For French of Paris was to her unknow.
				

				At meatĂŤ was she well y-taught withal;
				

				She let no morsel from her lippĂŤs fall,
				

				Nor wet her fingers in her saucĂŤ deep.
				

				Well could she carry a morsel, and well keep,
				

				That no droppĂŤ ne fell upon her breast.
				

				In courtesy was set full much her lest.44
				

				Her over-lippĂŤ wiped she so clean,
				

				That in her cup there was no farthing45 seen
				

				Of greasĂŤ, when she drunken had her draught;
				

				Full seemĂŤly after her meat she raught:46
				

				And sickerly she was of great disport,47
				

				And full pleasĂĄnt, and amiable of port,
				

				And pained her to counterfeitĂŤ cheer
				

				Of court,48 and be estately of mannĂŠre,
				

				And to be holden digne49 of reverence.
			

			
				But for to speaken of her consciĂŠnce,
				

				She was so charitable and so pitous,50
				

				She wouldĂŤ weep if that she saw a mouse
				

				Caught in a trap, if it were dead or bled.
				

				Of smallĂŤ houndĂŤs had she, that she fed
				

				With roasted flesh, and milk, and wastel bread.51
				

				But sore she wept if one of them were dead,
				

				Or if men smote it with a yardĂŤ52 smart:
				

				And all was conscience and tender heart.
				

				Full seemly her wimple y-pinched was;
				

				Her nose tretis;53 her eyen gray as glass;54
				

				Her mouth full small, and thereto soft and red;
				

				But sickerly she had a fair forehĂŠad.
				

				It was almost a spannĂŤ broad I trow;
				

				For hardily she was not undergrow.55
				

				Full fetis56 was her cloak, as I was ware.
				

				Of small corĂĄl about her arm she bare
				

				A pair of beadĂŤs, gauded all with

chapters.title: The Knightâs Tale
chapters.content:
The Knightâs Tale277
			
				Whilom,278 as oldĂŤ stories tellen us,
				

				There was a duke that hightĂŤ279 Theseus.
				

				Of Athens he was lord and governor,
				

				And in his timĂŤ such a conqueror
				

				That greater was there none under the sun.
				

				Full many a richĂŤ country had he won.
				

				What with his wisdom and his chivalry,
				

				He conquerâd all the regne of Feminie,280
				

				That whilom was y-cleped Scythia;
				

				And weddedĂŤ the Queen Hippolyta,
				

				And brought her home with him to his country
				

				With muchel281 glory and great solemnity,
				

				And eke her youngĂŤ sister Emily,
				

				And thus with victâry and with melody
				

				Let I this worthy Duke to Athens ride,
				

				And all his host, in armĂŤs him beside.
			

			
				And certes, if it nâere282 too long to hear,
				

				I would have told you fully the mannĂŠre,
				

				How wonnen283 was the regne of Feminie,
				

				By Theseus, and by his chivalry;
				

				And of the greatĂŤ battle for the nonce
				

				Betwixt AthenĂŤs and the Amazons;
				

				And how assieged was Hippolyta,
				

				The fairĂŤ hardy queen of Scythia;
				

				And of the feast that was at her wedding,
				

				And of the tempest at her homecoming.
				

				But all these things I must as now forbear.
				

				I have, God wot, a largĂŤ field to ear;284
				

				And weakĂŤ be the oxen in my plough;
				

				The remnant of my tale is long enow.
				

				I will not letten eke none of this rout.285
				

				Let every fellow tell his tale about,
				

				And let see now who shall the supper win.
				

				There as I left,286 I will again begin.
			

			
				This Duke, of whom I makĂŤ mentioĂşn,
				

				When he was come almost unto the town,
				

				In all his weal287 and in his mostĂŤ pride,
				

				He was ware, as he cast his eye aside,
				

				Where that there kneeled in the highĂŤ way
				

				A company of ladies, tway and tway,
				

				Each after other, clad in clothĂŤs black:
				

				But such a cry and such a woe they make,
				

				That in this world nâis creatĂşre living,
				

				That heardĂŤ such another waimenting.288
				

				And of this crying would they never stenten,289
				

				Till they the reinĂŤs of his bridle henten.290
				

				âWhat folk be ye that at mine homecoming
				

				Perturben so my feastĂŤ with crying?â
				

				Quoth Theseus; âHave ye so great envĂ˝
				

				Of mine honoĂşr, that thus complain and cry?
				

				Or who hath you misboden,291 or offended?
				

				Do tellĂŤ me, if it may be amended;
				

				And why that ye be clad thus all in black?â
			

			
				The oldest lady of them all then spake,
				

				When she had swooned, with a deadly cheer,292
				

				That it was ruthĂŤ293 for to see or hear.
				

				She saidĂŤ; âLord, to whom fortĂşne hath given
				

				Victâry, and as a conqueror to liven,
				

				Nought grieveth us your glory and your honoĂşr;
				

				But we beseechen mercy and succĂłur.
				

				Have mercy on our woe and our distress;
				

				Some drop of pity, through thy gentleness,
				

				Upon us wretched women let now fall.
				

				For certĂŤs, lord, there is none of us all
				

				That hath not been a duchess or a queen;
				

				Now be we caitives,294 as it is well seen:
				

				Thanked be Fortune, and her falsĂŤ wheel,
				

				That none estate ensureth to be wele.295
				

				And certes, lord, tâ abiden your presĂŠnce
				

				Here in this temple of the goddess Clemence
				

				We have been waiting all this fortĂŤnight:
				

				Now help us, lord, since it lies in thy might.
			

			
				âI, wretched wight, that weep and wailĂŤ thus,
				

				Was whilom wife to king Capaneus,
				

				That starf296 at Thebes, cursed be that day:
				

				And allĂŤ we that be in this array,
				

				And maken all this lamentatioĂşn,
				

				We losten all our husbands at that town,
				

				While that the siegĂŤ thereabouten lay.
				

				And yet the oldĂŤ Creon, well-away!
				

				That lord is now of Thebes the city,
				

				Fulfilled of ire and of iniquity,
				

				He for despite, and for his tyranny,
				

				To do the deadĂŤ bodies villainy,297
				

				Of all our lordĂŤs, which that been y-slaw,298
				

				Hath all the bodies on an heap y-draw,
				

				And will not suffer them by none assent
				

				Neither to be y-buried, nor y-brent,299
				

				But maketh houndĂŤs eat them in despite.â
				

				And with that word, withoutĂŤ more respite
				

				They fallen groff,300 and cryden piteously;
				

				âHave on us wretched women some mercy,
				

				And let our sorrow sinken in thine heart.â
			

			
				This gentle Duke down from his courser start
				

				With heartĂŤ piteous, when he heard them speak.
				

				Him thoughtĂŤ that his heart would all to-break,
				

				When he saw them so piteous and so mate,301
				

				That whilom weren of so great estate.
				

				And in his armĂŤs he them all up hent,302
				

				And them comfĂłrted in full good intent,
				

				And swore his oath, as he was truĂŤ knight,
				

				He wouldĂŤ do so farforthly his might303
				

				Upon the tyrant Creon them to wreak,304
				

				That all the people of GreecĂŤ shouldĂŤ speak,
				

				How Creon was of Theseus y-served,
				

				As he that had his death full well deserved.
				

				And right anon withoutĂŤ more abode305
				

				His banner he displayâd, and forth he rode
				

				To Thebes-ward, and all his host beside:
				

				No ner306 AthenĂŤs would he go nor ride,
				

				Nor take his easĂŤ fully half a day,
				

				But onward on his way that night he lay:
				

				And sent anon Hippolyta the queen,
				

				And Emily her youngĂŤ sister sheen307
				

				Unto the town of Athens for to dwell:
				

				And forth he rit;308 there is no more to tell.
			

			
				The red statĂşe of Mars with spear and targe
				

				So shineth in his whitĂŤ banner large,
				

				That all the fieldĂŤs glitter up and down:
				

				And by his banner borne is his pennon
				

				Of gold full rich, in which there was y-beat309
				

				The Minotaur310 which that he slew in Crete.
				

				Thus rit this Duke, thus rit this conquerour,
				

				And in his host of chivalry the flower,
				

				Till that he came to Thebes, and alight
				

				Fair in a field, there as he thought to fight.
				

				But shortly for to speaken of this thing,
				

				With Creon, which that was of Thebes king,
				

				He fought, and slew him manly as a knight
				

				In plain batĂĄille, and put his folk to flight:
				

				And by assault he won the city after,
				

				And rent adown both wall, and spar, and rafter;
				

				And to the ladies he restored again
				

				The bodies of their husbands that were slain,
				

				To do obsĂŠquies, as was then the guise.311
			

			
				But it were all too long for to devise312
				

				The greatĂŤ clamour, and the waimenting,313
				

				Which that the ladies made at the brenning314
				

				Of the bodiĂŤs, and the great honour
				

				That Theseus the noble conqueror
				

				Did to the ladies, when they from him went:
				

				But shortly for to tell is mine intent.
			

			
				When that this worthy Duke, this Theseus,
				

				Had Creon slain, and wonnen ThebĂŠs thus,
				

				Still in the field he took all night his rest,
				

				And did with all the country as him lest.315
				

				To ransack in the tas316 of bodies dead,
				

				Them for to strip of harness and of weed,317
				

				The pillers318 did their business and cure,
				

				After the battle and discomfiture.
				

				And so befell, that in the tas they found,
				

				Through girt with many a grievous bloody wound,
				

				Two youngĂŤ knightĂŤs ligging by and by319
				

				Both in one armĂŤs,320 wrought full richĂŤly:
				

				Of whichĂŤ two, Arcita hight that one,
				

				And he that other hightĂŤ Palamon.
				

				Not fully quick, nor fully dead they were,

chapters.title: The Millerâs Tale
chapters.content:
The Millerâs Tale
			
				The Prologue
				
					When that the Knight had thus his talĂŤ told,
					

					In all the rout was neither young nor old,
					

					That he not said it was a noble story,
					

					And worthy to be drawen to memĂłry;907
					

					And namĂŤly the gentles every one.908
					

					Our Host then laughâd and swore, âSo may I gon,909
					

					This goes aright; unbuckled is the mail;910
					

					Let see now who shall tell another tale:
					

					For truĂŤly this game is well begun.
					

					Now telleth ye, Sir Monk, if that ye conne,911
					

					Somewhat, to quiten912 with the KnightĂŤâs tale.â
					

					The Miller that fordrunken was all pale,913
					

					So that unnethes914 upon his horse he sat,
					

					He would avalen915 neither hood nor hat,
					

					Nor abide916 no man for his courtesy,
					

					But in PilatĂŤâs voice917 he gan to cry,
					

					And swore by armĂŤs, and by blood, and bones,
					

					âI can a noble talĂŤ for the nones,918
					

					With which I will now quite919 the KnightĂŤâs tale.â
					

					Our Host saw well how drunk he was of ale,
					

					And said; âRobin, abide, my levĂŤ920 brother,
					

					Some better man shall tell us first another:
					

					Abide, and let us workĂŤ thriftily.â921
					

					âBy GoddĂŤâs soul,â quoth he, âthat will not I,
					

					For I will speak, or ellĂŤs go my way!â
					

					Our Host answerâd; âTell on a devil way;922
					

					Thou art a fool; thy wit is overcome.â
					

					âNow hearken,â quoth the Miller, âall and some:
					

					But first I make a protestatioĂşn.
					

					That I am drunk, I know it by my sounâ:
					

					And therefore if that I misspeak or say,
					

					Wite923 it the ale of Southwark, I you pray:
					

					For I will tell a legend and a life
					

					Both of a carpenter and of his wife,
					

					How that a clerk hath set the wrightĂŤâs cap.â924
					

					The Reeve answĂŠrâd and saidĂŤ, âStint thy clap,925
					

					Let be thy lewĂŤd drunken harlotry.
					

					It is a sin, and eke a great folly
					

					To apeiren926 any man, or him defame,
					

					And eke to bringĂŤ wives in evil name.
					

					Thou mayâst enough of other thingĂŤs sayn.â
					

					This drunken Miller spake full soon again,
					

					And saidĂŤ, âLevĂŤ brother OsĂŤwold,
					

					Who hath no wifĂŤ, he is no cuckĂłld.
					

					But I say not therefore that thou art one;
					

					There be full goodĂŤ wivĂŤs many one.
					

					Why art thou angry with my talĂŤ now?
					

					I have a wife, pardie, as well as thou,
					

					Yet nâold927 I, for the oxen in my plough,
					

					Taken upon me morĂŤ than enough,
					

					To deemen928 of myself that I am one;
					

					I will believĂŤ well that I am none.
					

					An husband should not be inquisitive
					

					Of GoddĂŤâs privity, nor of his wife.
					

					So he may findĂŤ GoddĂŤâs foison929 there,
					

					Of the remnant needeth not to enquĂŠre.â
				

				
					What should I more say, but that this MillĂŠre
					

					He would his wordĂŤs for no man forbear,
					

					But told his churlish930 tale in his mannĂŠre;
					

					Me thinketh, that I shall rehearse it here.
					

					And therefore every gentle wight I pray,
					

					For GoddĂŤâs love to deem not that I say
					

					Of evil intent, but that I must rehearse
					

					Their tales all, be they better or worse,
					

					Or ellĂŤs falsen931 some of my mattere.
					

					And therefore whoso list it not to hear,
					

					Turn oâer the leaf, and choose another tale;
					

					For he shall find enough, both great and smale,
					

					Of storial932 thing that toucheth gentiless,
					

					And eke morality and holiness.
					

					Blame not me, if that ye choose amiss.
					

					The Miller is a churl, ye know well this,
					

					So was the Reeve, with many other moâ,
					

					And harlotry933 they toldĂŤ bothĂŤ two.
					

					Avise you934 now, and put me out of blame;
					

					And eke men should not make earnest of game.935

chapters.title: The Millerâs Tale
chapters.content:
The Millerâs Tale
			
				The Prologue
				
					When that the Knight had thus his talĂŤ told,
					

					In all the rout was neither young nor old,
					

					That he not said it was a noble story,
					

					And worthy to be drawen to memĂłry;907
					

					And namĂŤly the gentles every one.908
					

					Our Host then laughâd and swore, âSo may I gon,909
					

					This goes aright; unbuckled is the mail;910
					

					Let see now who shall tell another tale:
					

					For truĂŤly this game is well begun.
					

					Now telleth ye, Sir Monk, if that ye conne,911
					

					Somewhat, to quiten912 with the KnightĂŤâs tale.â
					

					The Miller that fordrunken was all pale,913
					

					So that unnethes914 upon his horse he sat,
					

					He would avalen915 neither hood nor hat,
					

					Nor abide916 no man for his courtesy,
					

					But in PilatĂŤâs voice917 he gan to cry,
					

					And swore by armĂŤs, and by blood, and bones,
					

					âI can a noble talĂŤ for the nones,918
					

					With which I will now quite919 the KnightĂŤâs tale.â
					

					Our Host saw well how drunk he was of ale,
					

					And said; âRobin, abide, my levĂŤ920 brother,
					

					Some better man shall tell us first another:
					

					Abide, and let us workĂŤ thriftily.â921
					

					âBy GoddĂŤâs soul,â quoth he, âthat will not I,
					

					For I will speak, or ellĂŤs go my way!â
					

					Our Host answerâd; âTell on a devil way;922
					

					Thou art a fool; thy wit is overcome.â
					

					âNow hearken,â quoth the Miller, âall and some:
					

					But first I make a protestatioĂşn.
					

					That I am drunk, I know it by my sounâ:
					

					And therefore if that I misspeak or say,
					

					Wite923 it the ale of Southwark, I you pray:
					

					For I will tell a legend and a life
					

					Both of a carpenter and of his wife,
					

					How that a clerk hath set the wrightĂŤâs cap.â924
					

					The Reeve answĂŠrâd and saidĂŤ, âStint thy clap,925
					

					Let be thy lewĂŤd drunken harlotry.
					

					It is a sin, and eke a great folly
					

					To apeiren926 any man, or him defame,
					

					And eke to bringĂŤ wives in evil name.
					

					Thou mayâst enough of other thingĂŤs sayn.â
					

					This drunken Miller spake full soon again,
					

					And saidĂŤ, âLevĂŤ brother OsĂŤwold,
					

					Who hath no wifĂŤ, he is no cuckĂłld.
					

					But I say not therefore that thou art one;
					

					There be full goodĂŤ wivĂŤs many one.
					

					Why art thou angry with my talĂŤ now?
					

					I have a wife, pardie, as well as thou,
					

					Yet nâold927 I, for the oxen in my plough,
					

					Taken upon me morĂŤ than enough,
					

					To deemen928 of myself that I am one;
					

					I will believĂŤ well that I am none.
					

					An husband should not be inquisitive
					

					Of GoddĂŤâs privity, nor of his wife.
					

					So he may findĂŤ GoddĂŤâs foison929 there,
					

					Of the remnant needeth not to enquĂŠre.â
				

				
					What should I more say, but that this MillĂŠre
					

					He would his wordĂŤs for no man forbear,
					

					But told his churlish930 tale in his mannĂŠre;
					

					Me thinketh, that I shall rehearse it here.
					

					And therefore every gentle wight I pray,
					

					For GoddĂŤâs love to deem not that I say
					

					Of evil intent, but that I must rehearse
					

					Their tales all, be they better or worse,
					

					Or ellĂŤs falsen931 some of my mattere.
					

					And therefore whoso list it not to hear,
					

					Turn oâer the leaf, and choose another tale;
					

					For he shall find enough, both great and smale,
					

					Of storial932 thing that toucheth gentiless,
					

					And eke morality and holiness.
					

					Blame not me, if that ye choose amiss.
					

					The Miller is a churl, ye know well this,
					

					So was the Reeve, with many other moâ,
					

					And harlotry933 they toldĂŤ bothĂŤ two.
					

					Avise you934 now, and put me out of blame;
					

					And eke men should not make earnest of game.935

chapters.title: The Millerâs Tale
chapters.content:
The Millerâs Tale
			
				The Prologue
				
					When that the Knight had thus his talĂŤ told,
					

					In all the rout was neither young nor old,
					

					That he not said it was a noble story,
					

					And worthy to be drawen to memĂłry;907
					

					And namĂŤly the gentles every one.908
					

					Our Host then laughâd and swore, âSo may I gon,909
					

					This goes aright; unbuckled is the mail;910
					

					Let see now who shall tell another tale:
					

					For truĂŤly this game is well begun.
					

					Now telleth ye, Sir Monk, if that ye conne,911
					

					Somewhat, to quiten912 with the KnightĂŤâs tale.â
					

					The Miller that fordrunken was all pale,913
					

					So that unnethes914 upon his horse he sat,
					

					He would avalen915 neither hood nor hat,
					

					Nor abide916 no man for his courtesy,
					

					But in PilatĂŤâs voice917 he gan to cry,
					

					And swore by armĂŤs, and by blood, and bones,
					

					âI can a noble talĂŤ for the nones,918
					

					With which I will now quite919 the KnightĂŤâs tale.â
					

					Our Host saw well how drunk he was of ale,
					

					And said; âRobin, abide, my levĂŤ920 brother,
					

					Some better man shall tell us first another:
					

					Abide, and let us workĂŤ thriftily.â921
					

					âBy GoddĂŤâs soul,â quoth he, âthat will not I,
					

					For I will speak, or ellĂŤs go my way!â
					

					Our Host answerâd; âTell on a devil way;922
					

					Thou art a fool; thy wit is overcome.â
					

					âNow hearken,â quoth the Miller, âall and some:
					

					But first I make a protestatioĂşn.
					

					That I am drunk, I know it by my sounâ:
					

					And therefore if that I misspeak or say,
					

					Wite923 it the ale of Southwark, I you pray:
					

					For I will tell a legend and a life
					

					Both of a carpenter and of his wife,
					

					How that a clerk hath set the wrightĂŤâs cap.â924
					

					The Reeve answĂŠrâd and saidĂŤ, âStint thy clap,925
					

					Let be thy lewĂŤd drunken harlotry.
					

					It is a sin, and eke a great folly
					

					To apeiren926 any man, or him defame,
					

					And eke to bringĂŤ wives in evil name.
					

					Thou mayâst enough of other thingĂŤs sayn.â
					

					This drunken Miller spake full soon again,
					

					And saidĂŤ, âLevĂŤ brother OsĂŤwold,
					

					Who hath no wifĂŤ, he is no cuckĂłld.
					

					But I say not therefore that thou art one;
					

					There be full goodĂŤ wivĂŤs many one.
					

					Why art thou angry with my talĂŤ now?
					

					I have a wife, pardie, as well as thou,
					

					Yet nâold927 I, for the oxen in my plough,
					

					Taken upon me morĂŤ than enough,
					

					To deemen928 of myself that I am one;
					

					I will believĂŤ well that I am none.
					

					An husband should not be inquisitive
					

					Of GoddĂŤâs privity, nor of his wife.
					

					So he may findĂŤ GoddĂŤâs foison929 there,
					

					Of the remnant needeth not to enquĂŠre.â
				

				
					What should I more say, but that this MillĂŠre
					

					He would his wordĂŤs for no man forbear,
					

					But told his churlish930 tale in his mannĂŠre;
					

					Me thinketh, that I shall rehearse it here.
					

					And therefore every gentle wight I pray,
					

					For GoddĂŤâs love to deem not that I say
					

					Of evil intent, but that I must rehearse
					

					Their tales all, be they better or worse,
					

					Or ellĂŤs falsen931 some of my mattere.
					

					And therefore whoso list it not to hear,
					

					Turn oâer the leaf, and choose another tale;
					

					For he shall find enough, both great and smale,
					

					Of storial932 thing that toucheth gentiless,
					

					And eke morality and holiness.
					

					Blame not me, if that ye choose amiss.
					

					The Miller is a churl, ye know well this,
					

					So was the Reeve, with many other moâ,
					

					And harlotry933 they toldĂŤ bothĂŤ two.
					

					Avise you934 now, and put me out of blame;
					

					And eke men should not make earnest of game.935

chapters.title: The Reeveâs Tale
chapters.content:
The Reeveâs Tale
			
				The Prologue
				
					When folk had laughed all at this nice case
					

					Of Absolon and Hendy Nicholas,
					

					DiversĂŤ folk diversĂŤly they said,
					

					But for the morĂŤ part they laughâd and playâd;1189
					

					And at this tale I saw no man him grieve,
					

					But it were only OsĂŤwold the Reeve.
					

					Because he was of carpentĂŠrĂŤâs craft,
					

					A little ire is in his heartĂŤ laft;1190
					

					He gan to grudge1191 and blamed it a lite.1192
					

					âSo thĂŠ I,â1193 quoth he, âfull well could I him quite1194
					

					With blearing1195 of a proudĂŤ millerâs eye,
					

					If that me list to speak of ribaldry.
					

					But I am old; me list not play for age;1196
					

					Grass time is done, my fodder is now forĂĄge.
					

					This whitĂŤ top1197 writeth mine oldĂŤ years;
					

					Mine heart is also moulded1198 as mine hairs;
					

					And I do fare as doth an open-erse;1199
					

					That ilkĂŤ1200 fruit is ever longer werse,
					

					Till it be rotten in mullok or in stre.1201
					

					We oldĂŤ men, I dread, so farĂŤ we;
					

					Till we be rotten, can we not be ripe;
					

					We hop1202 away, while that the world will pipe;
					

					For in our will there sticketh aye a nail,
					

					To have an hoary head and a green tail,
					

					As hath a leek; for though our might be gone,
					

					Our will desireth folly ever-in-one:1203
					

					For when we may not do, then will we speak,
					

					Yet in our ashes cold does firĂŤ reek.1204
					

					Four gledĂŤs1205 have we, which I shall devise,1206
					

					Vaunting, and lying, anger, covetĂ­se.1207
					

					These fourĂŤ sparks belongen unto eld.
					

					Our oldĂŤ limbĂŤs well may be unweld,1208
					

					But will shall never fail us, that is sooth.
					

					And yet have I alway a coltĂŤâs tooth,1209
					

					As many a year as it is passed and gone
					

					Since that my tap of life began to run;
					

					For sickerly,1210 when I was born, anon
					

					Death drew the tap of life, and let it gon:
					

					And ever since hath so the tap y-run,
					

					Till that almost all empty is the tun.
					

					The stream of life now droppeth on the chimb.1211
					

					The silly tonguĂŤ well may ring and chime
					

					Of wretchedness, that passed is full yore:1212
					

					With oldĂŤ folk, save dotage, is no more.â1213
				

				
					When that our Host had heard this sermoning,
					

					He gan to speak as lordly as a king,
					

					And said; âTo what amounteth all this wit?
					

					What? shall we speak all day of holy writ?
					

					The devil made a ReevĂŤ for to preach,
					

					As of a souter1214 a shipman, or a leach.1215
					

					Say forth thy tale, and tarry not the time:
					

					Lo here is Deptford, and âtis half past prime:1216
					

					Lo Greenwich, where many a shrew is in.
					

					It were high time thy talĂŤ to begin.â
				

				
					âNow, sirs,â quoth then this OsĂŤwold the Reeve,
					

					âI pray you all that none of you do grieve,
					

					Though I answĂŠr, and somewhat set his hove,1217
					

					For lawful is force off with force to shove.1218
					

					This drunken miller hath y-told us here
					

					How that beguiled was a carpentĂŠre,
					

					ParĂĄventure in scornâ âfor I am one:
					

					And, by your leave, I shall him quite anon.
					

					Right in his churlish termĂŤs will I speakâ â
					

					I pray to God his neckĂŤ might to-break.
					

					He can well in mine eyĂŤ see a stalk,1219
					

					But in his own he cannot see a balk.â

					He can well in mine eyĂŤ see a stalk,1219

chapters.title: The Cookâs Tale
chapters.content:
The Cookâs Tale
			
				The Prologue
				
					The Cook of London, while the Reeve thus spake,
					

					For joy he laughâd and clappâd him on the back:
					

					âAha!â quoth he, âfor ChristĂŤâs passiĂłn,
					

					This Miller had a sharp conclusiĂłn,
					

					Upon this argument of herbergage.1362
					

					Well saidĂŤ Solomon in his languĂĄge,
					

					Bring thou not every man into thine house,
					

					For harbouring by night is periloĂşs.
					

					Well ought a man avised for to be1363
					

					Whom that he brought into his privity.
					

					I pray to God to give me sorrow and care
					

					If ever, since I hightĂŤ1364 Hodge of Ware,
					

					Heard I a miller better set a-werk;1365
					

					He had a jape1366 of malice in the derk.
					

					But God forbid that we should stintĂŤ1367 here,
					

					And therefore if ye will vouchsafe to hear
					

					A tale of me, that am a poorĂŤ man,
					

					I will you tell as well as eâer I can
					

					A little jape that fell in our citĂ˝.â
				

				
					Our Host answĂŠrâd and said; âI grant it thee.
					

					Roger, tell on; and look that it be good,
					

					For many a pasty hast thou letten blood,
					

					And many a Jack of Dover1368 hast thou sold,
					

					That had been twicĂŤ hot and twicĂŤ cold.
					

					Of many a pilgrim hast thou ChristĂŤâs curse,
					

					For of thy parsley yet fare they the worse.
					

					That they have eaten in thy stubble goose:
					

					For in thy shop doth many a fly go loose.
					

					Now tell on, gentle Roger, by thy name,
					

					But yet I pray thee be not wroth for game;1369
					

					A man may say full sooth in game and play.â
					

					âThou sayst full sooth,â quoth Roger, âby my fay;
					

					But sooth play quad play,1370 as the Fleming saith,
					

					And therefore, Harry Bailly, by thy faith,
					

					Be thou not wroth, else we departĂŤ1371 here,
					

					Though that my tale be of an hostelĂŠre.1372
					

					But natheless, I will not tell it yet,
					

					But ere we part, y-wis1373 thou shalt be quit.â
					

					And therewithal he laughâd and madĂŤ cheer,1374
					

					And told his tale, as ye shall after hear.
